content.text: What it is

A RACI matrix for agent programs maps every significant decision to four types of participants: Responsible (who does the work), Accountable (who owns the outcome and has final say — exactly one per decision), Consulted (who must provide input before the decision), and Informed (who must be told after the decision). Decision rights specify the scope of authority each role holds — what they can decide unilaterally, what they need approval for, and what they have no authority over. Together, a role taxonomy, RACI matrix, and decision rights table form the operating system for an AI program, converting an org chart into a functioning governance structure.
Why it matters

Agent programs involve more decision types than traditional software projects: model selection, data handling, prompt versioning, responsible AI assessment, production deployment approval, incident response, and business value measurement. Without a RACI, these decisions default to whoever has the most organizational capital at the time — a pattern that produces inconsistent outcomes, relitigated decisions, and resentment from teams who feel they're excluded from decisions that affect them. A well-designed RACI is also an accountability map: when an AI incident occurs, the RACI tells you immediately who owned the decision that led to it, which is essential for root-cause governance and for regulatory responses.
Benefits
Eliminates decision gridlock by clarifying who can make which calls without requiring escalation or committee approval.
Creates clear accountability for AI outcomes — when a model underperforms or causes harm, there is a named accountable owner, not a diffuse team.
Speeds up delivery by delegating low-risk decisions to squads while concentrating review resources on high-risk decisions.
Provides a communication tool for onboarding new team members — the RACI is often the fastest way to orient a new hire to how the program operates.
Enables regulatory compliance demonstration — auditors can see exactly who approved a high-risk model deployment and what consultation process was followed.
Trade-offs and limitations
RACI matrices become bloated and unused when they try to cover every micro-decision — limit to decisions with material consequences or governance implications.
The matrix reflects power dynamics as much as logic; initial drafts often over-concentrate decision rights in the CAIO or CoE Director, requiring deliberate negotiation to delegate appropriately.
Agentic AI introduces novel decision types (agent autonomy scope, tool permissions, memory retention) that have no precedent in traditional software RACI frameworks.
RACI without process is incomplete — it specifies who decides but not how (what information must be reviewed, what criteria apply), which still leaves room for inconsistent decisions.
How it works

The core roles in an enterprise agent program are: Chief AI Officer (CAIO) — ultimate accountability for AI strategy and risk; CoE Director — operational accountability for governance and standards; Platform Engineering Lead — accountability for infrastructure decisions; Squad Product Owner — accountability for use-case delivery within a given squad; BU AI Lead — accountability for business domain alignment and BU stakeholder management; and Responsible AI Lead — accountability for bias, fairness, and ethical risk assessments.

Decision rights are structured as a hierarchy: the CoE charter establishes the CAIO's overall authority, the CoE's binding mandate covers a defined set of decisions, and below that, each role has a documented scope of autonomous decision-making. The key principle is single accountability per decision — every row in the RACI matrix has exactly one A (Accountable). Multiple Rs (Responsible) are acceptable and often appropriate for complex decisions that require multiple people to execute.

For agentic AI programs specifically, the RACI must address decisions that don't exist in traditional ML programs: what actions an agent is permitted to take autonomously (tool use scope), how much context an agent may retain between sessions (memory governance), what constitutes a material prompt change requiring re-approval vs. a minor edit within squad authority, and who decides when to roll back an agent that is producing unexpected outputs in production.
Architecture

attrs.alt: RACI matrix for key decisions in agent programs across six roles: CAIO, CoE Director, Platform Lead, Squad PO, BU AI Lead, RAI Lead
attrs.title: RACI Matrix for Agent Program Decisions

content.text: The matrix illustrates the distribution of accountability across eight representative decision types. Notice that AI strategy and annual roadmap carries A at the CAIO level — this is a strategic decision that cannot be delegated. High-risk production deployment approval is Accountable at CAIO but Responsible at CoE Director, meaning the CoE Director executes the review while the CAIO retains final accountability. Low-risk decisions like agent prompt changes for deployed models are fully delegated to the Squad PO with only notification to other roles.
When to use it

A formal RACI is necessary from the first moment a second team joins the AI program — before that, a single team's informal norms are sufficient. The RACI becomes critical when: two teams are making conflicting decisions about the same domain, when escalation to the CAIO is happening for decisions that should be decided lower, or when onboarding new team members consistently requires extensive orientation to 'how decisions work here'.
How to implement
Identify the 15–25 decision types that are most consequential for your agent program — focus on decisions that have caused confusion, conflict, or delay in the past 6 months.
List the roles (not individuals) that participate in these decisions — roles must be organizational positions, not people, so the RACI survives personnel changes.

Draft the initial matrix and check: every row has exactly one A, no row is all C and I (someone must be responsible for executing), and no role has A on more than 70% of rows (over-concentration of accountability).
Workshop the draft with representatives of each role — surface disagreements, particularly where two roles both believe they hold the A.
For each A decision, document the minimum information that must be available to the accountable party before a decision is made — this bridges RACI and process.
Include agent-specific decision categories: tool use scope, memory retention policy, autonomous action limits, and rollback authority.
Publish the RACI alongside the CoE charter and review it quarterly for the first year, then annually thereafter.
Examples

# RACI enforcement: check that a decision has proper authorization before proceeding
from enum import Enum
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional

class RACIRole(Enum):
    CAIO = "caio"
    COE_DIRECTOR = "coe_director"
    PLATFORM_LEAD = "platform_lead"
    SQUAD_PO = "squad_po"
    BU_AI_LEAD = "bu_ai_lead"
    RAI_LEAD = "rai_lead"

# Decision registry: maps decision types to their RACI assignments
DECISION_REGISTRY = {
    "prod_deploy_high_risk": {
        "accountable": RACIRole.CAIO,
        "responsible": [RACIRole.COE_DIRECTOR, RACIRole.RAI_LEAD],
        "consulted": [RACIRole.PLATFORM_LEAD, RACIRole.SQUAD_PO],
        "informed": [RACIRole.BU_AI_LEAD],
        "minimum_approvals": [RACIRole.COE_DIRECTOR, RACIRole.RAI_LEAD],
        "requires_accountable_sign_off": True,
    },
    "prod_deploy_low_risk": {
        "accountable": RACIRole.SQUAD_PO,
        "responsible": [RACIRole.SQUAD_PO],
        "consulted": [RACIRole.BU_AI_LEAD],
        "informed": [RACIRole.COE_DIRECTOR],
        "minimum_approvals": [],  # Self-certification
        "requires_accountable_sign_off": False,
    },
    "llm_vendor_selection": {
        "accountable": RACIRole.CAIO,
        "responsible": [RACIRole.COE_DIRECTOR, RACIRole.PLATFORM_LEAD],
        "consulted": [RACIRole.SQUAD_PO, RACIRole.BU_AI_LEAD, RACIRole.RAI_LEAD],
        "informed": [],
        "minimum_approvals": [RACIRole.COE_DIRECTOR, RACIRole.PLATFORM_LEAD],
        "requires_accountable_sign_off": True,
    },
    "prompt_change_minor": {
        "accountable": RACIRole.SQUAD_PO,
        "responsible": [RACIRole.SQUAD_PO],
        "consulted": [RACIRole.BU_AI_LEAD],
        "informed": [RACIRole.COE_DIRECTOR],
        "minimum_approvals": [],
        "requires_accountable_sign_off": False,
    },
}

@dataclass
class DecisionRequest:
    decision_type: str
    approvals_received: list[RACIRole]
    accountable_signed: bool

def can_proceed(req: DecisionRequest) -> tuple[bool, str]:
    spec = DECISION_REGISTRY.get(req.decision_type)
    if not spec:
        return False, f"Unknown decision type: {req.decision_type}"
    
    missing = [r for r in spec["minimum_approvals"] if r not in req.approvals_received]
    if missing:
        return False, f"Missing approvals from: {[r.value for r in missing]}"
    
    if spec["requires_accountable_sign_off"] and not req.accountable_signed:
        return False, f"Requires sign-off from accountable: {spec['accountable'].value}"
    
    return True, "Decision authorized to proceed"

# Test
req = DecisionRequest(
    decision_type="prod_deploy_high_risk",
    approvals_received=[RACIRole.COE_DIRECTOR, RACIRole.RAI_LEAD],
    accountable_signed=True  # CAIO signed off
)
ok, msg = can_proceed(req)
print(f"{ok}: {msg}")
# True: Decision authorized to proceed

# Agent-specific decision rights: agentic autonomy scope
# These decision types have no parallel in traditional ML programs

agent_decision_rights:

  tool_use_scope:
    description: What tools may the agent invoke autonomously vs. with human approval?
    low_risk_tools:  # Squad PO decides autonomously
      - web_search
      - internal_knowledge_base_read
      - calendar_read
      - document_summarization
    medium_risk_tools:  # BU AI Lead + CoE Director approve
      - email_send
      - crm_update
      - database_read_only
      - external_api_read
    high_risk_tools:  # CoE Director + RAI Lead + CAIO sign-off
      - payment_processing
      - database_write
      - external_api_write
      - code_execution
      - file_system_write
    prohibited_tools:  # No approval path -- hardcoded blocked
      - production_database_delete
      - user_impersonation
      - network_configuration_change

  memory_retention:
    session_memory:  # Discarded after session -- no approval needed
      max_duration: session
      approver: none
    short_term_user_memory:  # Retained across sessions for same user
      max_duration: 30d
      approver: bu_ai_lead
      requires_user_consent: true
    long_term_behavioral_memory:  # Persistent user behavioral profile
      max_duration: indefinite
      approver: coe_director + rai_lead
      requires_user_consent: true
      requires_data_governance_review: true

  autonomous_action_limits:
    max_sequential_actions_no_human_check: 5
    max_financial_impact_usd_autonomous: 1000
    rollback_authority: squad_po  # Can roll back without CoE approval
Real-world example

A global professional services firm's AI program ran into a recurring conflict: the CoE Director believed they had authority to reject agent deployments that didn't meet evaluation thresholds, while individual squad product owners believed deployment decisions were within their squad's autonomy once use cases were approved at intake. This ambiguity caused three projects to be delayed when the CoE Director rejected near-complete agents that squads believed were ready for production.

The RACI workshop that resolved this conflict ran for two half-day sessions. The outcome was a tiered deployment decision: for low-risk agents, the Squad PO has full deployment authority with a self-certification checklist (the CoE Director is only Informed). For medium-risk agents, the CoE Director reviews against defined evaluation criteria and has veto authority. For high-risk agents, the CoE Director and RAI Lead both review, and the CAIO must sign off. Critically, the evaluation criteria for each tier were defined in the same workshop, so the CoE Director's 'veto' was constrained to objective criteria, not subjective judgment.

After implementing the tiered RACI, 68% of deployment decisions were handled at the squad level without CoE involvement. The CoE Director's review load dropped from approximately 2 full days per week to 4 hours per week, freeing capacity for strategic work. Mean time from approval to production dropped 34% as a result of removing unnecessary escalation steps.
Common mistakes
Having more than one Accountable per decision row — this is the most common RACI error and produces the exact ambiguity the RACI is meant to eliminate.
Mapping decisions to individuals rather than roles — the RACI must survive personnel changes, which means assigning to the CoE Director role, not to Jane Smith.
Over-consulting: marking five roles as Consulted when only two actually have relevant expertise creates consultation fatigue and delays decisions.

Not including agentic-specific decision categories (tool use scope, memory retention, autonomous action limits) — these are novel governance decisions without precedent in traditional software RACI frameworks.
Publishing the RACI without enforcement mechanisms — a RACI that teams can ignore is not a governance document, it's a suggestion.
Best practices
Keep the RACI to 20–30 decision rows maximum — above this it becomes a bureaucratic document rather than an operational tool.
Define the minimum information requirements for each Accountable decision — who decides is useless without specifying what information they must review.
Enforce the RACI programmatically where possible — automate approval workflows so the system prevents unauthorized deployments, not just policy documents.
Review the RACI after every significant AI incident — incidents reveal gaps and ambiguities in decision rights that weren't visible before the fact.
Pair the RACI with decision documentation: every A-level decision should produce a brief written record of what was decided, why, and who was consulted.

Use DACI (Driver, Approver, Contributors, Informed) as an alternative when 'driver' is more useful than 'responsible' — DACI emphasizes who is moving the decision forward, which maps well to project-based agent delivery.
Performance and scalability

The RACI's performance impact is measured in decision throughput: how many decisions per week are made at the right level without unnecessary escalation. A well-designed RACI should move 70%+ of decisions to squad level, with fewer than 20% requiring CoE Director involvement, and fewer than 5% escalating to CAIO. If actual escalation patterns deviate significantly from this distribution, the RACI is either too conservative (too many low-risk items escalating) or too permissive (high-risk items not getting appropriate review).

As the program scales to 10+ squads, the RACI must evolve to include federated decision rights for BU-level governance structures. A flat RACI that routes all medium-risk decisions to a single CoE Director becomes a bottleneck at scale. The solution is a tiered RACI where BU AI Leads hold accountable authority for medium-risk decisions within their BU, with central CoE review reserved for cross-BU or high-risk items.

Strengths

Best For

Doesn't specify process or criteria
Establishing role clarity

Includes criteria weighting for complex choices

Security and reliability

Security decisions must be explicitly represented in the RACI. Key security decisions for agent programs include: who approves an agent's tool use scope (particularly for write operations), who approves the data classification ceiling for a given agent's context, who authorizes an agent to access production systems, and who is accountable for security incident response when an agent is involved. Without these in the RACI, security governance defaults to ad-hoc, which is the condition that creates the most exploitable gaps in enterprise AI programs.

Reliability governance through the RACI means assigning accountability for defining and enforcing SLAs for each production agent, for deciding when an agent should be rolled back vs. hot-patched, and for approving changes to monitoring thresholds. These decisions are often absent from traditional software RACI frameworks but are essential for agentic AI given the difficulty of predicting agent behavior at deployment time.
Key takeaways
Every RACI row must have exactly one Accountable — this is the non-negotiable structural rule; multiple Accountable parties create the ambiguity the RACI is meant to eliminate.
Assign to roles, not individuals — the RACI must survive personnel changes to provide durable governance.
Agent programs require novel decision categories — tool use scope, memory retention policy, and autonomous action limits have no precedent in traditional software governance frameworks.
Enforce the RACI programmatically in approval workflows — a policy-only RACI is a suggestion, not a governance control.
70%+ of decisions should be delegated to squad level in a well-designed RACI — a RACI that concentrates authority at the CoE Director level is a governance bottleneck, not a governance structure.
Review the RACI after every AI incident — incidents reveal governance gaps that pre-emptive design consistently misses.
